prompt: At the most basic level, what does a large language model do when it drafts a commit message from a diff?
choices: Looks the message up in a connected fact database
Predicts the next token repeatedly, one token at a time
Executes the diff as program code
Searches the live internet for the best matching message

prompt: Which of the following are genuine limitations you must design around when using an LLM in a software workflow? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Hallucination — it can confidently suggest an API method that does not exist
Knowledge cutoff — it may not know a framework version released after training
Non-determinism — the same prompt may yield different output run to run
It cannot produce any output longer than a single line

prompt: What is the difference between training and inference?
choices: Training happens on every API call; inference happens once
Training learns the model's weights once; inference uses those frozen weights to answer each prompt your tools send
They are two names for the same process
Inference permanently updates the model's knowledge with each new question

prompt: A team is choosing models for the coding phase: (a) generating commit messages on every push, and (b) reviewing an architecture proposal for hidden coupling. Which pairing of model choices is most appropriate?
choices: Largest model for both, to be safe
Small/cheap model for commit messages; large/capable model for the architecture review
Large/capable model for commit messages; small/cheap model for the architecture review
Smallest model for both, to minimize cost regardless of task

prompt: A task is simple but extremely high-volume and latency-sensitive, and a small error rate is cheap to catch downstream. Which model choice is the better *trade-off* here?
choices: A small, fast, cheap model — the volume and latency outweigh a marginal accuracy gain
The largest frontier model, since more capability is always safer
Fine-tune a mid-size model on the task before launching
A different random model per request to average out errors
